title: Haiti TPS Bill Heads to Senate; Trump Threatens Veto, Leaving 500,000+ Haitians in Limbo
detail: Following the US House passage of Haiti TPS extension legislation on April 16, 2026 (vote: 224–204, with 10 Republicans voting yes), the bill moved to the Senate where it faces an uncertain path. The Trump administration signaled presidential opposition, with the administration indicating it would veto any measure that blocked TPS terminations — threatening the legislation even if the Senate passes it. The bill, led by Rep. Ayanna Pressley (D-MA) and advanced via discharge petition to bypass committee leadership, would extend Temporary Protected Status for Haitians through 2029, protecting an estimated 500,000–700,000 qualifying Haitians in the United States from deportation. Faith-based organizations, immigrant advocacy groups, and the Haitian diaspora immediately urged Senate leadership to bring the measure to the floor. Critics of mass deportation emphasized that Haiti remains in acute crisis: gangs control approximately 90% of Port-au-Prince, 5.8 million Haitians face food insecurity, voter registration for August 2026 elections has been indefinitely postponed, and the Gang Suppression Force has yet to reach operational strength. Deportees arriving in Haiti would face immediate exposure to gang violence, forced recruitment, extortion, or worse. The Senate Republican majority had not scheduled debate on the bill as of April 19, 2026. Advocacy groups noted that courts had already issued preliminary injunctions against earlier TPS termination actions pending legal review, meaning existing legal protections remained in place for now.

media.caption: US House votes 224-204 to extend TPS for Haitians in rebuke of Trump administration

title: Haiti-DR Air Link Deal: Cap-Haïtien Airport and Three Dominican Airports Named as First Routes
detail: Reporting on April 19, 2026 confirmed operational details of the Haiti-Dominican Republic airspace reopening agreement announced April 17: the initial routes will connect Cap-Haïtien's Hugo Chavez International Airport in northern Haiti to three Dominican airports, likely including Las Américas International Airport in Santo Domingo. The air link had been suspended since March 2024 when the Dominican Republic closed its airspace due to escalating gang violence in Haiti. For Cap-Haïtien — Haiti's second-largest city and gateway to the historic Citadelle Laferrière — the deal is particularly significant, as it provides an alternate international connection independent of Port-au-Prince's Toussaint Louverture Airport, which has faced periodic gang threats and operational disruptions. Business groups in both countries welcomed the announcement as a step toward restoring commerce, diaspora travel, and humanitarian logistics. The Dominican Republic has simultaneously been completing construction of a 174-kilometer concrete border wall along the shared frontier to prevent gang spillover while pursuing economic reengagement. Trade between the two nations was valued at approximately $1.8 billion annually before the current crisis, though cross-border commerce had been severely disrupted since 2022.

media.caption: Dominican Republic and Haiti reopen airspace in May, restoring flights
